content.text:
{
  "reference": "John 3:16",
  "source_translation": "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.",
  "kardia_translation": "For God so enacted His unconditional covenant commitment toward the world that He gave His one and only Son, so that everyone who entrusts themselves wholly to Him — staking their entire existence on who He is — would not perish but would possess the life of the age to come.",
  "key_term": {
    "term": "loved",
    "hebrew": "אהבה / חֶסֶד",
    "notes": "The Greek agapao used here draws on the Hebrew covenantal love concept. It is not primarily emotional affection but the enacted, unconditional commitment of a covenant-keeping God who binds Himself to the object of His love regardless of the worthiness of the recipient. The world here is not a worthy covenant partner — it is estranged and rebellious — which makes the covenantal direction of this love all the more theologically explosive."
  },
  "hebrew_category": "Covenantal Love — ahavah and chesed. In Hebrew thought, love directed from God toward an unworthy recipient is not sentiment. It is sovereign covenant initiative. God binds Himself. The giving of the Son is not an emotional response — it is the enacted proof of a commitment that originated in God's own character before the world had any claim on Him.",
  "why_this_matters": "Standard translations render this as emotional affection — God had warm feelings toward the world. But the Hebrew thought world behind this Greek text understands love as covenant enacted, not emotion felt. The theological weight shifts entirely: this is not God responding to something lovable in the world. This is God initiating an unconditional covenant commitment toward a world that had forfeited every claim on Him. The giving of the Son is the covenant act that proves the commitment is real. Believes is also flattened in English — the Greek pisteuo carries the Hebrew batach weight of full trust, reliance, and staked existence — not mere cognitive agreement.",
  "extra_notes": [
    "The Greek monogenes rendered only Son carries the weight of unique, one-of-a-kind — not merely an only child in a family sense but the singular unrepeatable Son who shares the Father's own being. This weight is often lost in translation.",
    "Eternal life in Greek is zoe aionios — literally the life of the age, referring to the resurrection life of the coming age breaking into the present. It is not primarily about duration but about quality and source — the life that belongs to God Himself, now made available.",
    "The structure of the verse moves from covenant commitment to covenant act to covenant outcome — a fully Hebraic movement from character to deed to result."
  ]
}
